summary: Cathy O'Neil examines how Lending Club, with Larry Summers on its board, circumvents the Equal Credit Opportunity Act by using alternative data sources like browsing history and Facebook connections to make lending decisions. Unlike traditional credit scores that must be behavior-based under FTC regulations, these algorithmic models can incorporate any data they can find online, effectively enabling discrimination that would be illegal for traditional lenders. She draws parallels to how employers use social media to perform informal background checks that bypass Fair Credit Reporting Act protections. The core problem is that people denied loans or jobs by these opaque algorithms never learn why, eliminating any possibility of recourse or appeal.
key_points: Lending Club uses browsing history, social media connections, and data from companies like Acxiom to score borrowers, bypassing Equal Credit Opportunity Act restrictions that require behavior-based credit scoring
Some lending algorithms evaluate your Facebook friends to determine if you 'run with the right crowd' before approving loans
Traditional credit and employment laws provide consent, disclosure, and recourse - but these new algorithmic systems operate invisibly with no appeals process
The same regulatory arbitrage applies to hiring: employers can glean legally protected information through casual social media searches without any of the FCRA's protections
Big investors are funding these companies precisely because bypassing credit regulation is profitable
money_quotes: Turns out that bypassing credit score regulation is great for business, so of course.
You can now blame your friends if you don't get that loan!
Maybe I should just go ahead and stop being friends with anyone who might put my electronic credit score at risk?
I think the silent failure is the scariest part for me – people who don't get jobs won't know why.
As applicants, we don't know if employers are looking, we're not privy to what they see, and we have no way to appeal.
From my perspective it's a logical continuation of his career of deregulation and/or bypassing of vital regulation to enrich himself.
Of course this rant is predicated on the assumption that we think anti-discrimination laws are a good thing. In an ideal world, of course, we wouldn't need them. But that's not where we live.
key_insight: New algorithmic lending and hiring systems achieve regulatory arbitrage by using data sources that allow discrimination the law prohibits, while their opacity denies victims any knowledge of or recourse against the decisions made about them.
